subject: Surgery
question:
A 55-year-old male presented with verrucous carcinoma around the glans of the penis.
Examination reveals that the inguinal lymph nodes are not enlarged. What is the appropriate
management for this patient?

[Surgery-NEET-2023-1-image]
explanation:
The most appropriate management for this patient with verrucous carcinoma around the glans of
the penis is partial penectomy.
Carcinoma of the penis is commonly a squamous cell carcinoma. It arises from the skin of the
glans penis or the prepuce. The lesion can be flat or warty in appearance.
• T1- confined to the skin
• T2- invades the corpus spongiosum or cavernosum.
• T3- invades the urethra
• T4- invades adjacent structures.
The first lymph node to be involved is the superficial inguinal lymph node and thus, the sentinel
lymph node biopsy (SLNB) is helpful for evaluating the spread of penile cancer.
Tumors of low-grade and low-stage are managed by Mohs’ surgery. Tumors involving the glans
penis require glansectomy, and in advanced cases require partial penectomy.
Other options:
Option A: In penile carcinoma, total amputation of the penis is done if the stump is < 2cm after
resection of the tumor.
Options B, and C: Topical 5-fluorouracil cream and CO2 laser ablation are preferred in case of
penile intraepithelial neoplasia.

[Surgery-NEET-2023-1-exp-image]

subject: Surgery
question:
Identify the procedure being performed in the image given below.
5034

[Surgery-NEET-2023-2-image]

options.c: Intraosseous route access for giving IV fluids

explanation:
5047
The procedure being performed in the above image is intraosseous route access for transfusing IV
fluids in the anterior proximal tibia.
The intraosseous route is an alternative to the intravenous (IV) route in the transfusion of fluids in
shock. The marrow of long bones has a rich network of vessels that drain into a central venous
canal, emissary veins, and ultimately the central circulation. So, the bone marrow functions as a
non-collapsible venous access route.
Intraosseous cannulation is recommended for patients in whom IV access proves difficult or
unattainable. (e.g., in cardiopulmonary arrest). In such a case, if venous access is not available
within 1 min, an intraosseous needle should be placed in the anterior proximal tibia. During a
resuscitation procedure, all anesthetic drugs and fluids can be administered through intraosseous
cannulae.
Insertion sites for intraosseous infusion:
• In the proximal tibia, 1-2 cm anteromedial from the tibial tuberosity
• Distal tibia at the junction of the medial malleolus and the shaft of the tibia
• Distal one-third of the femur
The response time to drug administration in intraosseous infusion is close to the IV route. Fluids
should be given as boluses using a 50 mL syringe.
The image below shows the procedure of intraosseous cannulation.

[Surgery-NEET-2023-2-exp-image]

subject: Surgery
question:
A diabetic patient presents with sudden-onset perineal pain. On examination, foul-smelling
discharge, and necrotic tissue as seen in the image is noted. Which of the following is true
about the given condition?

[Surgery-NEET-2023-3-image]

options.a:
Anti-gas gangrene serum indicated for all cases
5035
options.b: Urinary diversion is the next step

explanation:
The given condition is likely to be Fournier gangrene. It is a mixed flora aerobic and anaerobic
infection.
Fournier's gangrene is a polymicrobial infection and a type of necrotizing fasciitis of the
perineum, external genitalia, and perianal region. Risk factors include immunocompromised
states such as HIV, alcoholism, and diabetes. The infection may be precipitated by minor
5048
procedures in the perineal area, such as a bruise, scratch, urethral dilatation, injection of
hemorrhoids, or opening of a periurethral abscess. The infection spreads along the dartos, Scarpa,
and Colles fascia.
The sequence of events that lead to the development of gangrene are as follows: A mixed infection
of the aerobic and anaerobic bacteria  → fulminating inflammation
of subcutaneous tissue → obliterative arteritis of the arterioles that supply the scrotal skin →
gangrene.
Clinical features of Fournier gangrene are scrotal pain, fever, prostration, and pallor. Cellulitis
spreads rapidly and may be associated with eschars, necrosis, flaking skin, and crepitus.
The testis is usually not affected.
Diagnosis is majorly clinical and very rarely made by laboratory or imaging findings.
The steps in the management of this condition:
• IV fluids
• IV antibiotics
• Urgent surgical excision followed by skin grafting in later stages. Wide surgical excision is often
necessary.
When the infection has been controlled, a vacuum-assisted dressing may be helpful. Skin grafting
is essential for a patient surviving an acute episode. Despite the best therapy, mortality rates are
around 50%.
Other options:
Option A: Anti-gas gangrene serum is a treatment for gas gangrene that is of historical importance
and is no longer used. Treatment of gas gangrene is now done with antibiotics.
Option B: While urinary diversion and other supportive measures are useful in the management
of the condition, it is not the next best step.
Option C: The blood supply of the testes is separate from that of the overlying skin, and hence,
testes can be preserved during debridement.

subject: Surgery
question:
Which of the following is the most common complication following ligation of the first vessel
during abdominoperineal resection for rectal carcinoma?

options.a: Parasympathetic - bladder dysfunction and retrograde ejaculation.
options.c: Sympathetic - retrograde ejaculation and bladder dysfunction.
options.d: Sympathetic - Impotence and loss of cutaneous sensation in perineal region

explanation:
The first vessel that is usually ligated during abdominoperineal resection (APR) is the inferior
mesenteric artery (IMA). A high inferior mesenteric artery ligation during abdominoperineal
resection can injure the superior hypogastric nerve, resulting in sympathetic dysfunction
and leading to retrograde ejaculation and bladder dysfunction.
Abdominoperineal resection is the complete excision of the rectum, anal canal, and sphincter
apparatus. This is done with the creation of a permanent colostomy. This is commonly preferred
in elderly patients with distal rectal cancer with poor sphincter function. In these patients, the end
colostomy offers a better quality of life.
Complications:
• Higher recurrence rates compared to low anterior resection.
5049
• Anastomotic leaks or dehiscences
• Necrosis of the transposed colon
• Bleeding
• Strictures
The sympathetic innervation of the rectum is from L1, L2, and L3, which together form the
superior hypogastric plexus. They divide into left and right hypogastric nerves at the level of the
sacral promontory supplying the rectum and the genitourinary system. A high IMA ligation during
surgery can result in sympathetic dysfunction causing retrograde ejaculation.
Injury to the pelvic plexus and nervi erigentes, during the division along the pelvic side wall, can
cause erectile dysfunction, impotence, and atonic bladder. Injury to the periprostatic plexus can
lead to sexual and bladder dysfunction.

subject: Surgery
question:
A patient presents to the clinic with the lesion given in the image. He had a traumatic injury
to the chest one year ago. What is the most likely diagnosis?

[Surgery-NEET-2023-5-image]
explanation:
The image showing a large area of scar tissue is suggestive of a keloid.
A keloid refers to excess accumulation of collagen at the scar site with growth of the scar tissue
beyond the boundaries of the original wound. It does not regress. The patient's history of
traumatic injury to the chest one year ago, followed by the development of an expanding scar, is
suggestive of a keloid. The keloid is often glossy in appearance and rubbery in texture. They are
composed of thick bundles of collagen.
Treatment of keloids usually involves intralesional injections of triamcinolone. Multiple doses are
required at intervals over several weeks and may result in flattening but rarely does the wound
narrow.
Option A: A hemangioma is a vascular tumor that arises from the endothelium. They typically
present as red or pinkish macules or raised lesions.
Option B: Hypertrophic scars, unlike keloids, remain within the boundaries of the original wound
and tend to regress over time. Since this scar has failed to regress over 1 year and has grown
beyond the original wound margins, it is more likely to be a keloid.
Option D: Neurofibromas are benign tumors arising from Schwann cells. They usually present as
soft, flesh-colored nodules, often in association with neurofibromatosis type 1, a genetic disorder.
The patient's history of chest trauma and the appearance of the lesion make neurofibroma an
unlikely diagnosis in this case.

subject: Surgery
question:
A 45-year-old male underwent bilateral laparoscopic hernia repair for inguinal hernia.
Postoperatively, he complained of pain in the right thigh. Which of the following nerve
entrapment leads to this symptom?
explanation:
Entrapment of the lateral cutaneous nerve of the thigh is the most likely cause of pain in this
patient with history of laparoscopic hernia repair.
The laparoscopic approach is preferred for bilateral or recurrent inguinal hernias.
Transabdominal preperitoneal (TAPP) repair and total extraperitoneal (TEP) repair are the
methods commonly used.
5050
The nerves commonly injured in laparoscopic repair are the genitofemoral and lateral femoral
cutaneous nerves of the thigh. Patients present with acute neuropathic pain along the dermatomal
distribution. Injury to the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve results in meralgia paresthetica.
Treatment consists of rest, ice, NSAIDs, physical therapy, and possible local corticosteroid and
anesthetic injection. Drugs like gabapentin can be used.
In laparoscopic TAPP repair, to reduce the chances of nerve injury during mesh placement, tacks
are ideally placed above the iliopubic tract to avoid nerve injury.
Other options:
Option A: Ilioinguinal nerve may be commonly injured during open hernia repair. It leads to
sensory disturbances and pain over the skin of the genitalia and upper medial thigh.
Option B: Iliohypogastric nerve can be injured while making an incision in the right iliac fossa
during hernia surgery.
Option C: Femoral nerve can be compressed by retroperitoneal tumors or hemorrhage in patients
on anticoagulants or bleeding diathesis. It presents as wasting and weakness of the quadriceps
femoris muscle making the patient difficult to walk. Pain and paraesthesia are seen along the
region of the distribution of the nerve.

subject: Surgery
question:
A patient was brought to the ER following a road traffic accident. On examination, the patient
opens his eyes to a painful stimulus, speaks inappropriately, and withdraws his limbs to a
painful stimulus. What is his GCS score?
explanation:
The GCS score of this patient: E2V3M4
• He opens his eyes to a painful stimulus - E2
• He produces inappropriate words only - V3
• He withdraws his limbs - M4

subject: Surgery
question:
A patient presented with fever and abdominal pain with jaundice. Investigations showed the
given findings. Which of the following statements is true regarding this condition?
5037

[Surgery-NEET-2023-8-image]

options.c: Angioembolization is the treatment of choice
options.d: Echinococcus species is involved in etiology

explanation:
The given CT image shows a cyst in the liver with serpentine linear membranes seen as wavy
membranes within the cyst is suggestive of hydatid disease.
Hydatid disease or echinococcosis is a zoonotic infection caused by the Echinococcus species
of flatworms. Its life cycle consists of dogs as definitive hosts and humans as
abberant intermediate hosts. Primary hydatid cysts most commonly occur in the liver. Most
patients are asymptomatic but when symptomatic, the patient presents with hepatomegaly, pain,
and obstructive jaundice.
The first/initial imaging test is USG, and a CT scan can be used to diagnose the condition.
CT scan shows the presence of a space-occupying lesion with a smooth outline and septa. CT scan
has an accuracy of 98% in the detection of hydatid cysts and high sensitivity to the detection of
daughter cysts and extra-hepatic cysts. On CT imaging, the serpent sign is seen as wavy
membranes within the cyst.
5051
The USG of an active hydatid cyst shows multiple septations and a rosette or honeycomb
appearance:

[Surgery-NEET-2023-8-exp-image]
The ball of wool sign is seen on USG when the contents settle in the dependent portion of the cyst,
as seen below:

[Surgery-NEET-2023-8-exp-image]
On USG, the water-lily sign is seen in hydatid infections when there is detachment of the endocyst
membrane, which results in floating membranes within the pericyst that mimic the appearance of
a water lily.
5052

[Surgery-NEET-2023-8-exp-image]
Other options:
Option A: While surgical treatment of hydatid cyst is useful and effective, alternatives like
percutaneous aspiration, injection, re-aspiration (PAIR) are equally effective and are currently
used.
Option B: IgG ELISA and indirect hemagglutinin assay are used for the diagnosis of hydatid cyst.
Option C: Angioembolization has no role in the treatment of hydatid cyst.

subject: Surgery
question:
A 40-year-old male patient presents with a swelling that has been slowly growing in the past 2
years. On examination, it was variable in consistency and fully mobile. What is the most likely
diagnosis?

[Surgery-NEET-2023-9-image]
explanation:
The given clinical scenario and image depict a likely case of parotid tumor, likely to be a
pleomorphic adenoma.
Pleomorphic adenoma is the most common benign salivary gland tumor. The most commonly
affected gland is the parotid gland. The superficial lobe is most commonly affected. It is also
called as mixed tumor since it has both epithelial and mesenchymal components. It most
commonly affects females around 40 years of age.
The common presenting feature is a painless swelling below, in front, or behind the ear. The
involvement of the deep lobe leads to dumbbell tumor – swelling in both the neck and oral cavity.
Specific features:
• Slowly growing benign tumor
• Unicentric in origin
• Potentially malignant
• Possibility of protrusion of fringes of the tumor mass through the capsule
• Radioresistant
• Usually affects adults, females > males
5053
• Painless swelling
• Well-defined margins
• Lobulated, firm, elastic-to-hard consistency
• Free from deeper structures
• No facial nerve involvement
• The deep lobe pushes tonsils and faucial pillars
Diagnosis is made by FNAC, which is considered the investigation of choice. Open biopsy
contraindicated due to chances of injury to the facial nerve and seedling of the tumor
Surgery is the mainstay of treatment - Superficial parotidectomy (Patey's operation). Radiotherapy
is considered in high-grade malignancies demonstrating extra-glandular disease, perineural
invasion, direct invasion of surrounding tissue, or regional metastases.
Pathology:
Gross features:
• The tumors are rounded, well-demarcated, measuring <6 cm in greatest dimension.
• They are usually encapsulated, except in some areas such as the palate.
• The cut surface is gray-white with myxoid and blue translucent areas of the
chondroid (cartilage-like) stroma.

[Surgery-NEET-2023-9-exp-image]
Microscopic features:
• The epithelial elements resemble ductal cells or myoepithelial cells. They are arranged as ducts,
acini, irregular tubules, strands, or sheets of cells.
• These epithelial elements are dispersed within a background of loose myxoid and hyaline
tissue containing islands of cartilage and, rarely, foci of bone.
The image below shows pleomorphic adenoma of the salivary gland consisting of mixed epithelial
(left) and mesenchymal cell components (right).
5054

[Surgery-NEET-2023-9-exp-image]
Dermoid cysts are slow-growing benign lesions. They are mostly seen on the scalp and skull. It is
made of hair, teeth, cartilage, and skin glands. Other common sites are the forehead,
postauricular, and lateral part of the eyebrow.
A sebaceous cyst is derived from hair follicle infundibulum or traumatic inclusion. The most
common site of a sebaceous cyst is the postauricular sulcus or below and behind the ear lobule.
Malignancy is rare.
Clinical features of a sebaceous cyst include:
• Benign swelling that is mobile, fluctuant, and fixed to the skin. It presents with a central
punctum in about 70% of cases.
• It is generally an asymptomatic, non-tender swelling. It can be painful and red if inflamed.
• It may ooze out a foul-smelling, cheese-like discharge.

subject: Surgery
question:
A patient who is a known case of alcohol dependence syndrome presents with sudden and
unintentional weight loss. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
The most likely diagnosis in this patient with a history of alcohol dependence, sudden
unintentional weight loss, and raised AFP is hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC).
HCC is the most common cause of primary liver cancer. It is more commonly seen in males, and
the peak age group is between 65-70 years. The main risk factor for HCC is cirrhosis and
associated chronic liver damage due to chronic infection by hepatitis B or C virus (HBV or HCV),
alcohol abuse, metabolic syndrome, or hemochromatosis. The incidence of HCC is increasing
worldwide. This is contributed by the increase in HCV and HBV infections, along with lifestyle
disorders like obesity. Obesity is associated with non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD). This
progresses on to non-alcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH) causing cirrhosis and hepatocellular
carcinoma. Thus, NASH and NAFLD are risk factors for HCC.
Regular surveillance, including liver imaging and monitoring of alpha-fetoprotein levels, is
recommended for high-risk populations, such as those with liver cirrhosis, chronic hepatitis B or
C infection, or significant alcohol consumption. Early detection of HCC can improve treatment
5055
outcomes and overall prognosis.
Very early HCC is treated by ablation. Early HCC with a single nodule is resected, but when there
are multiple nodules over 3 cm, ablation or transplantation are considered. Intermediate-stage
HCC is treated by chemoembolization. Transarterial chemoembolization (TACE) is used for
multinodular lesions.
Other Options:
Option A: Hepatic adenomas are benign liver tumors that are often associated with oral
contraceptive use and anabolic steroid use. The patient's history of alcohol dependence and high
alpha-fetoprotein levels are not consistent with hepatic adenoma. Additionally, hepatic adenomas
are usually asymptomatic and do not present with sudden unintentional weight loss.
Option B: Cholangiocarcinoma is a cancer that arises from the bile ducts within the liver. While
patients with alcohol dependence may have an increased risk of cholangiocarcinoma, the elevated
alpha-fetoprotein levels are not typical for this type of cancer. Cholangiocarcinomas usually
present with jaundice, pruritus, and abdominal pain, but sudden weight loss is less characteristic.
Option D: Alcoholic hepatitis is an inflammation of the liver caused by excessive alcohol
consumption. It usually presents with jaundice, fever, and abdominal pain. While the patient's
history of alcohol dependence might suggest alcoholic hepatitis, elevated alpha-fetoprotein levels
and unintentional weight loss are not typical for this condition. Moreover, the AST/ALT ratio is
usually greater than 2 in alcoholic hepatitis, but it is 0.5 in this case.

subject: Surgery
question:
A patient presented with features of chronic pancreatitis with recurrent attacks and has a 10
mm dilatation of the pancreatic duct with intraductal calculi present. Which of the following
is the surgery of choice?
explanation:
In this case of chronic pancreatitis with recurrent attacks, 10 mm dilatation of the pancreatic duct,
and intraductal calculi, longitudinal pancreaticojejunostomy (also known as Puestow procedure or
lateral pancreaticojejunostomy) is the surgery of choice. This procedure involves opening the
pancreatic duct along its length and connecting it to a loop of the jejunum, which helps to drain
the obstructed pancreatic duct and alleviate pain.
Patients with chronic pancreatitis mainly experience pain due to ductal hypertension or
parenchymal inflammation. Acute exacerbations in duct pressure lead to recurrent acute
attacks. The pancreatic duct dilation observed on pancreatography for chronic pancreatitis is
classically described as a chain of lakes, which reflects the presence of multiple dilations and
stenoses. Response to treatment has been found to be dependent upon the degree of preoperative
pancreatic fibrosis.
Longitudinal pancreaticojejunostomy, also known as the Puestow procedure or lateral
pancreaticojejunostomy, is a surgical treatment primarily used for chronic pancreatitis with a
dilated main pancreatic duct and intraductal calculi.
In longitudinal pancreaticojejunostomy, a longitudinal incision is made along the length of the
main pancreatic duct, typically from the tail to the head of the pancreas. The dilated pancreatic
duct is then opened, and any intraductal calculi or obstructions are removed. The opened duct is
then connected to a loop of the jejunum to create a side-to-side anastomosis. This allows for the
drainage of pancreatic secretions directly into the jejunum, bypassing any narrowed or obstructed
areas of the duct.
5056
This procedure can effectively relieve pain and improve the quality of life for patients with chronic
pancreatitis. However, it is important to note that it may not reverse the underlying pathology of
chronic pancreatitis or prevent the progression of the disease. It is also not suitable for all patients
with chronic pancreatitis, particularly those with a non-dilated pancreatic duct or isolated head
involvement.
Other Options:
Pancreaticoduodenectomy: This procedure, also known as Whipple procedure, involves removing
the head of the pancreas, duodenum, gallbladder, and part of the bile duct. It is typically
performed for pancreatic cancer or tumors in the head of the pancreas, not for chronic
pancreatitis with ductal obstruction and intraductal calculi.
ERCP and sphincterotomy: Endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) with
sphincterotomy is a procedure that involves opening the sphincter of Oddi to facilitate the
drainage of bile and pancreatic secretions. Although it may provide temporary relief, it is not a
definitive treatment for chronic pancreatitis with large ductal dilatation and intraductal calculi.
ERCP with sphincterotomy may be useful in some cases of chronic pancreatitis for the removal of
small pancreatic duct stones, but for large ductal dilatation and intraductal calculi, longitudinal
pancreaticojejunostomy is more appropriate.
Coring of pancreas head: Coring of the pancreas head, also known as pancreatic head resection or
Beger procedure, is a surgical technique used to treat chronic pancreatitis, particularly when the
inflammation and fibrosis predominantly affect the head of the pancreas. The procedure involves
removing the inflamed and fibrotic tissue from the head of the pancreas while preserving the
duodenum, bile duct, and the main pancreatic duct. Longitudinal pancreaticojejunostomy is a
more targeted and effective treatment for this patient's condition.

subject: Surgery
question:
A woman presented with a BIRADS-5 breast lesion. Which of the following is a good
prognostic factor for this lesion?
explanation:
ER positivity is a good prognostic factor in breast cancer.
The ER and PR are two steroid hormone receptors present in breast cancer. The expression of
these receptors indicates a favorable prognosis and response to hormonal therapy.
Other options:
Option A: The mutations in BRCA1 gene account for up to 40% of familial breast cancers. These
tumors are high-grade, hormone receptor–negative, and aneuploid and do not have a good
prognosis.
Option B: p53 is a tumor suppressor gene, which on mutation acts as an oncogene. p53 positivity
is associated with highly malignant breast cancers and has poor survival.
Option D: Ki-67 is used to access the proliferative index (activity) of the tumor. High Ki-67
indicates a poor prognosis.

subject: Surgery
question:
5039
A patient presents with sudden onset of chest pain shooting to the neck and interscapular
region. X-ray shows widened mediastinum. BP is 110/90 mmHg in the right upper limb and
160/100 mmHg in the left upper limb. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
5057
Sudden-onset severe chest pain, with a wide discrepancy in blood pressure between the two limbs,
and a widened mediastinum on x-ray point towards a diagnosis of aortic dissection.
It is initiated by an intimal tear, which allows blood to penetrate into the medial layer, producing
a cleavage plane (false lumen) between the inner two-thirds and outer one-third of the media. The
true lumen is separated from the false lumen by an intimomedial flap.
Classification of aortic dissection:
Acute aortic dissection can be classified by the original DeBakey system, which is based on the
location of the proximal tear and distal extent of the dissection flap, or the Stanford system, which
is based only on the location of the proximal tear.

[Surgery-NEET-2023-13-exp-image]
Clinically, patients present with tearing chest/back pain associated with diaphoresis, syncope,
weakness, and dyspnea or neurological manifestations.
On examination, unequal pulses or blood pressure in the extremities may be seen due to changes
of flow in the true and false lumens. There can be hypertension or hypotension, loss of pulses,
findings of aortic regurgitation, and pulmonary edema.
Imaging:
5058
Transesophageal echocardiography (TEE) is the investigation of choice in a hemodynamically
unstable patient. In hemodynamically stable patients, the diagnosis is established with a
contrast-enhanced CT scan. The characteristic diagnostic finding is a double-lumen aorta. The
image below shows aortic dissection in the thoracic aorta with the true lumen, as well as the false
lumen. The true lumen is often the smaller of the two because of being compressed by the false
lumen.
Acute coronary syndrome and acute pulmonary embolism may both present with sudden onset
chest pain but widened mediastinum and blood pressure discrepancy make aortic dissection more
likely. (Options A and B)
Both sudden onset chest pain and widened mediastinum may be seen in esophageal rupture but
blood pressure discrepancy between the upper limbs makes aortic dissection more likely. (Option
D)
Chest x-ray showing widened mediastinum in a patient with aortic dissection

dissection:
5059

subject: Surgery
question:
A patient presented with right hypochondriac pain. He had an episode of diarrhea 1 week
prior. CT scan of the abdomen reveals a liver abscess of around 25 ccs. What is the next step
in management?
explanation:
This patient with an abscess in the liver with a history of diarrhea most likely has an amebic liver
abscess. The initial treatment of choice for an amebic liver abscess is medical therapy
with metronidazole.
Ingestion of Entamoeba histolytica cysts causes intestinal diseases like diarrhea. Following this,
the trophozoites reach the liver mostly through the portal venous system and cause amebic liver
abscess. The common presenting features are right upper quadrant pain, tenderness, fever,
hepatomegaly, and diarrhea.
CT abdomen is helpful in differentiating amebic from a pyogenic abscess. A rim enhancement is
noted in case of a pyogenic abscess.
The mainstay of treatment for amebic abscesses is metronidazole, which is curative in more than
90% of patients. Luminal agents such as iodoquinol, paromomycin, or diloxanide furoate can be
administered to treat the carrier state.
Aspiration of abscess is recommended in case of
• Diagnostic uncertainty
• Failure to respond to metronidazole therapy
• Abscesses larger than 5 cm in diameter and in the left liver, since they are thought to carry a
high risk of rupture

subject: Surgery
question:
A 50-year-old patient with a history of trauma one week back presents with confusion and
ataxia. He is on treatment for alcohol dependence and his last intake was 2 weeks back. CT
scan reveals the following finding. What is the diagnosis?

[Surgery-NEET-2023-15-image]
5040
explanation:
The given clinical scenario and the plain CT scan showing a concavo-convex-shaped hematoma on
the left side with a midline shift to the right is suggestive of subdural hematoma (SDH).
5060
An acute SDH is the result of the accumulation of blood between the arachnoid membrane and
the dura. It is usually due to the rupture of bridging veins running from the cerebral cortex to the
dural sinuses. The level of consciousness is usually impaired at presentation and may deteriorate
further as the hematoma expands. Elderly and alcoholic patients are at a higher risk of acute SDH
formation after head trauma due to brain atrophy. On CT scan, acute SDH appears as a
hyperdense, crescent-shaped (concavo-convex) lesion.
Open craniotomy for the evacuation of acute SDH is indicated for the following:
• Thickness >1 cm
• Midline shift >5 mm
• Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) score drops by two or more points from the time of injury to
hospitalization.
Chronic subdural hematoma results from a hematoma that expands over days or weeks by
osmosis, ultimately producing symptoms of raised ICP or focal deficits. On a CT scan, diffuse
hypodensity overlying the brain surface would be seen.

subject: Surgery
question:
Which of the following is false about the given condition?

[Surgery-NEET-2023-16-image]

options.a: Repeated radical surgery every 24 hours may be required to adequately manage this condition
options.b: This is a case of Meleney's gangrene
options.c: The condition is caused by beta hemolytic streptococcus and may sometimes be polymicrobial
options.d: Hyperbaric oxygen has no role in the treatment of this condition

explanation:
The given image is likely that of Meleney's gangrene. Hyperbaric oxygen has been shown to
reduce mortality in patients with Meleney's gangrene and therefore plays a significant role in the
management of this condition.
Necrotizing fasciitis of the abdominal wall is known as Meleney's gangrene. It presents with
extensive necrosis of the abdominal wall, thrombosis of the microvasculature, and an acute
inflammatory infiltrate. Bullae develop on the abdominal wall, which progress to overt cutaneous
gangrene. The affected area is often painful, erythematous, and edematous, with possible systemic
signs of infection such as fever and malaise. Early recognition and aggressive treatment are
essential for a favorable outcome.
Management includes prompt surgical debridement of the necrotic tissue, broad-spectrum
antibiotics to cover the polymicrobial nature of the infection, and supportive measures such as
fluid resuscitation and correction of electrolyte imbalances. In some cases, hyperbaric oxygen
therapy may be utilized as an adjunctive treatment to improve tissue oxygenation and enhance the
healing process.
Other options:
Option A: Surgical excision of necrotic tissue is often required daily as the infection tends to
spread beyond the borders of the debridement wound.
Option C: Meleney's gangrene is caused by beta-hemolytic streptococci or Staphylococcus
aureus and may also be polymicrobial.

subject: Surgery
question:
A patient presents with a swelling in the groin region and it is diagnosed as a direct inguinal
hernia. Weakness in which of the following structures is most likely to cause this condition?
explanation:
A direct inguinal hernia is caused due to weakness in the conjoint tendon.
5061
The innermost layers of the lateral abdominal wall contain two muscles, namely internal oblique
and transverse abdominis. These muscle fibers arch over the deep inguinal ring from lateral to
medial, fuse, and become tendinous forming the conjoint tendon. The area below this arch lacks
muscle and contains external oblique aponeurosis and fascia transversalis, making it weak.
Direct inguinal hernia is common in elderly patients. It is seen medial to the inferior epigastric
vessels, in Hesselbach’s triangle. This area lacks muscle and contains only fascia.
Other options:
Option A: Pectineal ligament also known as Cooper's ligament is a strong fibrous band that
extends laterally along the pectineal line from the pectineal attachment of the lacunar ligament.
Option C: The inguinal ligament is external oblique aponeurosis that extends between the anterior
superior iliac spine and the pubic tubercle. On the point of attachment to the pubic tubercle, some
fibers pass upwards behind the superficial inguinal ring and external oblique aponeurosis to join
the rectus sheath and linea alba. These form the reflected part of the inguinal ligament.
Option D: Lacunar ligament is formed from the fibers of the medial end of the inguinal ligament
and the fibers from the fascia lata of the thigh. It forms the medial wall of the femoral canal.

[Surgery-NEET-2023-17-exp-image]

subject: Surgery
question:
A patient presents with sudden onset of hematemesis. He has been taking aspirin for his
arthritis and drinks alcohol occasionally. He gives a history of occasional abdominal pain. On
examination, there is no abdominal mass or tenderness. What is the likely diagnosis?
explanation:
This patient with a history of aspirin use, now presenting with abdominal pain and a sudden onset
of hematemesis most likely has a bleeding peptic ulcer.
Peptic ulcer disease (PUD) accounts for around 2/3rd of upper gastrointestinal bleeding. The
major causative factors are Helicobacter pylori and NSAIDs. Prostaglandins protect gastric and
duodenal mucosa from acid and pepsin. They increase mucin and bicarbonate secretion
and increase mucosal blood flow. They also promote epithelial cell proliferation. NSAIDs like
aspirin disrupt these naturally protective mechanisms and increase the risk of peptic ulcer
formation in the stomach and the duodenum.
5062
They present with abdominal pain, bleeding, or signs of peritonitis due to perforation. Significant
hemorrhage occurs when duodenal or gastric ulcers penetrate branches of the gastroduodenal or
left gastric arteries, respectively.
Other options:
Option A: Mallory-Weiss tears are partial-thickness tears of the mucosa and submucosa occurring
near the gastroesophageal junction. They occur in patients with a history of repeated emesis and
are classical in alcoholic patients after a period of intense retching and vomiting following binge
drinking.
Option B: Esophagitis commonly occurs due to repeated exposure of mucosa to the acidic gastric
secretions in gastroesophageal reflux disease. It results in chronic blood loss and commonly
presents as anemia or guaiac-positive stools. Ulcerations of superficial mucosa due to esophagitis
generally do not bleed acutely.
Option D: Esophageal varices commonly occur due to portal hypertension in patients with
cirrhosis. A history of liver disease or the stigmata of portal hypertension and cirrhosis would
suggest variceal bleeding.

subject: Surgery
question:
A construction worker came to the OPD complaining of white fingers. He has been working in
the cement and concrete industry and has been working on heavy machinery drills, wood,
and furniture polishing for 20 years. What is the most likely etiology for this condition?

options.a: Candidal infection of the fingers due to continuous exposure to water
options.c: Continuous exposure to cement and concrete
options.d: Continuous exposure to drills and machines

explanation:
The clinical vignette suggests the diagnosis of Raynaud's syndrome due to chronic exposure to
drills and machines (vibrating tools).
Raynaud's syndrome occurs due to peripheral vasospasm, and it is more common in the upper
extremities. It may occur due to exposure to various stimuli, including cold temperatures,
tobacco, or emotional stress, and certain occupational groups, such as those who use vibrating
tools, or it can be secondary to scleroderma, systemic lupus erythematosus, Sjogren's syndrome,
and antiphospholipid syndrome.
The pathophysiology behind Raynaud's syndrome is believed to be due to increased levels of
α2-adrenergic receptors and their hypersensitivity and also due to an abnormal thermoregulatory
response by the sympathetic nervous system.
A Raynaud's phenomenon attack is sudden, and it ranges from intense pallor (white attack) to
cyanosis (blue attack), followed by reactive hyperemia due to subsequent relaxation of blood
vessels. It typically affects fingers (distal areas), and the thumb is usually spared. If the thumb is
involved, it may indicate underlying secondary etiology. Other common complaints include pin
and needles sensation, numbness, or finger aches or pain. In severe attacks, digital ulcerations
and gangrene may develop.
Diagnosis is primarily based on the clinical history and physical examination. Nail fold capillary
microscopy can be helpful, particularly for identifying secondary Raynaud's phenomenon
associated with connective tissue diseases. Angiography can be done for patients with digital
ulcerations and gangrene.
Management is mainly conservative, and it includes wearing gloves, use of electric or chemically
activated hand warmers, avoiding occupational exposure to vibratory tools, abstinence from
tobacco, and relocating to a warmer, dryer climate.
Other management options:
5063
• Calcium channel–blockers such as diltiazem and nifedipine are the drugs of choice.
• Fluoxetine has been shown to reduce the frequency and duration of vasospastic episodes.
• Prostaglandins can be used in patients with severe symptoms.
Complications, including digital ulcerations and gangrene, can be surgically treated by
debridement and amputation, respectively.

subject: Surgery
question:
A 5-year-old child was admitted to the hospital for prolapsing rectal mass and painless rectal
bleeding. Histopathological examination reveals enlarged and inflamed glands filled with
mucin. What is the likely diagnosis?
explanation:
The given features are suggestive of a juvenile polyp, which are a type of hamartomatous polyp.
They are benign growths that are composed of a disorganized mixture of different types of tissues.
Juvenile polyps are malformations of epithelium and lamina propria commonly located in the
rectum. It is seen in children <5 years of age. The typical presentation is painless rectal
bleeding. Sometimes, there may be a proliferating rectal mass.
The disease may be sporadic or syndromic.
• Sporadic juvenile polyp occurs as a single mass and is called a retention polyp.
• Juvenile polyposis syndrome is characterized by multiple hamartomatous polyps, which may be
premalignant.
Morphologically, both of these types are indistinguishable.
• Gross appearance: They appear as pedunculated, smooth reddish lesions, and with cystic spaces.
• Microscopic appearance: The cysts appear as dilated glands filled with mucin and inflammatory
debris.

[Surgery-NEET-2023-20-exp-image]
Adenoma: Adenomas are benign tumors that arise from glandular tissue. While they may present
with rectal bleeding, they are not commonly seen in young children and are not typically
pedunculated like juvenile polyps.
5064
Choristoma: Choristomas are non-neoplastic masses that arise from heterotopic tissue, i.e., tissues
found in abnormal locations. They are not associated with juvenile polyps.

5042

explanation:
The given picture showing an ulcer with punched-out edges over a pressure point is most likely
that of a trophic ulcer/neuropathic ulcer.
A neuropathic ulcer is one that occurs as a result of peripheral neuropathy. In peripheral
neuropathy, lack of sensation over the pressure points leads to cumulative microtrauma, minor
wounds, or cuts that remain unnoticed but eventually develop into ulcers.
Features of a neuropathic ulcer include
• Painless ulcer
• Punched out ulcer with regular margins
• Often located on the plantar surface of the foot
• Ulcer margins show the presence of calluses
